content.text: Several production-ready vector databases exist, each with different deployment models, feature sets, and performance trade-offs. Choosing the right one is mostly a function of where your data lives, how many vectors you need, and whether you want to manage infrastructure. The six databases covered here represent the most commonly used options in agent and RAG deployments.

attrs.alt: Table comparing Qdrant, ChromaDB, Weaviate, Pinecone, pgvector, and LanceDB across hosting model, index type, filtering, quantisation support, and recommended use case.
attrs.title: Vector Database Landscape: Feature Comparison

content.text: ChromaDB runs in-process as a Python library — no Docker, no server, no configuration. It is ideal for prototyping and local development. The same client code also connects to a hosted Chroma server for production deployments.

# pip install chromadb voyageai
import chromadb
import voyageai

_CHROMA = chromadb.Client()               # in-memory (ephemeral)
# _CHROMA = chromadb.PersistentClient(path=".chroma")  # persists to disk
_VC     = voyageai.Client()
COLLECTION = _CHROMA.get_or_create_collection("agent_memory")


def chroma_upsert(texts: list[str], ids: list[str], metadatas: list[dict]) -> None:
    vecs = _VC.embed(texts, model="voyage-3", input_type="document").embeddings
    COLLECTION.upsert(
        ids=ids,
        embeddings=vecs,
        documents=texts,
        metadatas=metadatas,
    )


def chroma_search(
    query: str,
    where: dict | None = None,
    k: int = 5,
) -> list[dict]:
    q_vec = _VC.embed([query], model="voyage-3", input_type="query").embeddings[0]
    results = COLLECTION.query(
        query_embeddings=[q_vec],
        n_results=k,
        where=where,   # e.g. {"user_id": "alice"}
    )
    return [
        {"id": rid, "text": doc, "distance": dist}
        for rid, doc, dist in zip(
            results["ids"][0],
            results["documents"][0],
            results["distances"][0],
        )
    ]


# Example
chroma_upsert(
    texts=["Alice prefers dark mode.", "Bob uses a Mac."],
    ids=["mem_1", "mem_2"],
    metadatas=[{"user_id": "alice"}, {"user_id": "bob"}],
)
print(chroma_search("display settings", where={"user_id": "alice"}))

Pinecone is a fully managed cloud service — no Docker, no server management. The serverless tier charges per query rather than per reserved capacity, making it cost-effective for intermittent workloads. The trade-off is vendor lock-in and no local development mode.

# pip install pinecone-client voyageai
from pinecone import Pinecone, ServerlessSpec
import voyageai
import uuid

_PC = Pinecone(api_key="YOUR_PINECONE_KEY")
_VC = voyageai.Client()


def ensure_pinecone_index(name: str, dims: int = 1024) -> None:
    if name not in [i.name for i in _PC.list_indexes()]:
        _PC.create_index(
            name=name,
            dimension=dims,
            metric="cosine",
            spec=ServerlessSpec(cloud="aws", region="us-east-1"),
        )


def pinecone_upsert(index_name: str, texts: list[str], metadatas: list[dict]) -> None:
    ensure_pinecone_index(index_name)
    idx  = _PC.Index(index_name)
    vecs = _VC.embed(texts, model="voyage-3", input_type="document").embeddings
    idx.upsert(
        vectors=[
            {"id": str(uuid.uuid4()), "values": vec, "metadata": {"text": text, **meta}}
            for text, vec, meta in zip(texts, vecs, metadatas)
        ]
    )


def pinecone_search(
    index_name: str,
    query: str,
    filter_: dict | None = None,
    k: int = 5,
) -> list[dict]:
    idx   = _PC.Index(index_name)
    q_vec = _VC.embed([query], model="voyage-3", input_type="query").embeddings[0]
    resp  = idx.query(vector=q_vec, top_k=k, filter=filter_, include_metadata=True)
    return [{"id": m.id, "score": m.score, **m.metadata} for m in resp.matches]

LanceDB stores data in Apache Lance format — columnar, versioned, and directly readable from S3. It runs embedded in Python (like ChromaDB) but can also point at an S3 bucket, making it ideal for serverless functions that need persistent vector storage without a server.

Weaviate enforces a typed schema ("classes" with defined properties), supports multi-vector objects, and exposes a GraphQL API. It is well-suited for projects where data has rich structure that benefits from schema enforcement and graph-style traversal.

# pip install weaviate-client voyageai
import weaviate
import voyageai

_WC = weaviate.Client("http://localhost:8080")
_VC = voyageai.Client()

SCHEMA = {
    "class": "Memory",
    "properties": [
        {"name": "body",    "dataType": ["text"]},
        {"name": "user_id", "dataType": ["text"]},
    ],
    "vectorizer": "none",   # we supply our own vectors
}


def weaviate_setup() -> None:
    existing = {c["class"] for c in _WC.schema.get()["classes"]}
    if "Memory" not in existing:
        _WC.schema.create_class(SCHEMA)


def weaviate_upsert(texts: list[str], user_ids: list[str]) -> None:
    weaviate_setup()
    vecs = _VC.embed(texts, model="voyage-3", input_type="document").embeddings
    with _WC.batch as batch:
        batch.batch_size = 32
        for text, vec, uid in zip(texts, vecs, user_ids):
            batch.add_data_object(
                {"body": text, "user_id": uid},
                class_name="Memory",
                vector=vec,
            )


def weaviate_search(query: str, user_id: str, k: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    q_vec = _VC.embed([query], model="voyage-3", input_type="query").embeddings[0]
    result = (
        _WC.query.get("Memory", ["body", "user_id"])
        .with_near_vector({"vector": q_vec})
        .with_where({"path": ["user_id"], "operator": "Equal", "valueText": user_id})
        .with_limit(k)
        .do()
    )
    return result["data"]["Get"]["Memory"]
ChromaDB — zero setup, in-process, same client API for production.
Qdrant — best filter support, quantisation, and Python client ergonomics.
pgvector — add one extension, no new service.
Weaviate — GraphQL API and class-based schema.

Metadata filtering separates toy databases from production-grade ones. Querying by vector alone returns the K nearest neighbors globally — but agents need results scoped to a user, a project, or a time range. Each database implements pre-filtering or post-filtering differently, with significant performance implications.

# ChromaDB: metadata dict, arbitrary key/value
results = collection.query(
    query_embeddings=[q_vec],
    n_results=5,
    where={"user_id": {"$eq": "alice"}, "project": {"$in": ["proj-a", "proj-b"]}},
    where_document={"$contains": "budget"},   # full-text filter on document text
)

# Pinecone: sparse filter dict using Pinecone Filter Language
index.query(
    vector=q_vec,
    top_k=5,
    filter={"user_id": {"$eq": "alice"}, "timestamp": {"$gte": 1_700_000_000}},
    include_metadata=True,
)

# LanceDB: SQL WHERE clause — pushed down to Lance file format
tbl.search(q_vec).where("user_id = 'alice' AND score >= 0.8").limit(5).to_list()

# Weaviate: GraphQL nearVector + where filter
resp = wv_client.query.get("Chunk", ["text", "user_id"]).with_near_vector(
    {"vector": q_vec}
).with_where({
    "path": ["user_id"],
    "operator": "Equal",
    "valueString": "alice",
}).with_limit(5).do()

# Qdrant: payload filter — most expressive, supports geo/range/nested
from qdrant_client.models import Filter, FieldCondition, MatchValue, Range
qdrant.search(
    collection_name="chunks",
    query_vector=q_vec,
    query_filter=Filter(
        must=[
            FieldCondition(key="user_id", match=MatchValue(value="alice")),
            FieldCondition(key="timestamp", range=Range(gte=1_700_000_000)),
        ]
    ),
    limit=5,
)
Qdrant's payload filter is the most expressive, supporting range, geo, nested, and
clauses. ChromaDB's filter is simple but limited to flat key/value. LanceDB's SQL WHERE clause leverages the columnar Lance format for fast pre-filtering with zero overhead for unfiltered queries.
Agents ingest documents in bulk during initialization and incrementally during runs. Each database has different optimal batch sizes and async/sync APIs.

import asyncio
from typing import Any
import numpy as np

async def batch_upsert_pinecone(
    index,
    ids: list[str],
    vectors: list[list[float]],
    metadata: list[dict],
    batch_size: int = 100,
) -> None:
    """Pinecone recommends batches of 100 for free tier, up to 1000 for paid."""
    for start in range(0, len(ids), batch_size):
        batch = list(zip(
            ids[start:start + batch_size],
            vectors[start:start + batch_size],
            metadata[start:start + batch_size],
        ))
        index.upsert(vectors=batch)

def batch_upsert_qdrant(
    client,
    collection: str,
    ids: list[str],
    vectors: np.ndarray,
    payloads: list[dict],
    batch_size: int = 256,
) -> None:
    """Qdrant handles large batches efficiently; 256 is a safe default."""
    from qdrant_client.models import PointStruct
    for start in range(0, len(ids), batch_size):
        points = [
            PointStruct(id=ids[i], vector=vectors[i].tolist(), payload=payloads[i])
            for i in range(start, min(start + batch_size, len(ids)))
        ]
        client.upsert(collection_name=collection, points=points)

def batch_upsert_chroma(collection, ids, embeddings, docs, metadatas, batch_size=500):
    """ChromaDB: batch up to 41666 items; 500 is practical for memory."""
    for start in range(0, len(ids), batch_size):
        end = start + batch_size
        collection.add(
            ids=ids[start:end],
            embeddings=embeddings[start:end],
            documents=docs[start:end],
            metadatas=metadatas[start:end],
        )
with an Arrow table for maximum throughput — Lance's columnar format makes bulk appends very fast. Qdrant's
allows concurrent batches; for embeddings generated at >1000 req/s, Qdrant's gRPC interface halves latency compared to HTTP.
Qdrant, Weaviate, and ChromaDB all use HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) graphs internally. Understanding two key parameters prevents silent recall degradation.
Higher → better recall at high k

# Qdrant: configure HNSW on collection creation
from qdrant_client.models import VectorParams, Distance, HnswConfigDiff

qdrant.create_collection(
    collection_name="chunks",
    vectors_config=VectorParams(size=1024, distance=Distance.COSINE),
    hnsw_config=HnswConfigDiff(
        m=32,               # more edges — better recall for large corpora (>500k vectors)
        ef_construct=200,   # slower build, higher graph quality
        full_scan_threshold=10_000,  # below this count, brute-force beats HNSW
    ),
)

# Increase ef at query time for a high-stakes retrieval
qdrant.search(
    collection_name="chunks",
    query_vector=q_vec,
    limit=10,
    search_params={"hnsw_ef": 128},  # overrides collection default for this query
)

# ChromaDB: HNSW config via metadata
collection = client.create_collection(
    name="chunks",
    metadata={
        "hnsw:space": "cosine",
        "hnsw:construction_ef": 200,
        "hnsw:search_ef": 100,
        "hnsw:M": 32,
    },
)
For small agent memory stores (<50k vectors), defaults are fine. Once a collection exceeds 500k vectors, tuning
improves Recall@10 by 5–15% at the cost of 2× build time and ~25% more RAM for the HNSW graph.
Agents need to evict stale or superseded memories. Each database handles deletion differently, and understanding the API prevents memory leaks.
is the most ergonomic. Qdrant's payload-filter delete is atomic. ChromaDB and Pinecone require fetching IDs first, which limits bulk deletes — keep
or paginate to avoid missing records beyond the limit.
ChromaDB is the fastest path from zero to working vector search — pure Python, no server needed.
Qdrant provides the best feature set for production agent memory: payload filters, int8/binary quantisation, HNSW, and a clean Python client.
Pinecone eliminates all infrastructure management but adds vendor lock-in and no local dev mode.
All six databases use roughly the same pattern: embed text → upsert with metadata → ANN search with optional filter.
